steps.title: Focus on within-lake uniformity
steps.content: The passage explains why isotope abundance and activity ratios matter for sampling: if values are not uniform across one body of water, localized samples may be unreliable. Park et al. tested that concern by measuring U-238 concentration and the U-234/U-238 activity ratio at multiple depths and locations in two lakes. Their key finding was not a difference between the lakes, but no significant variation in values within the same lake.

steps.title: Keep the inference local
steps.content: Choice A turns the background concern into a broad recommendation for all scientists, not a conclusion from Park et al.'s specific lakes. Choice B misreads the result as consistency across different lakes, while the text emphasizes consistency within each lake. Choice D adds unsupported claims about the number of samples and about equal reliability for sediment transport and groundwater-quality research. Since values from multiple locations and depths within each studied lake were consistent, a localized sample from one of those lakes would likely represent the whole lake. The answer is C.
